content.text: Format consistency means every example in a few-shot prompt uses identical delimiters, field names, ordering, and whitespace. The model learns the output format by pattern-matching across the examples — if two examples use different separators or field names, the model does not infer a consistent rule; it treats the prompt as a noisy signal and may reproduce either pattern or invent a third one. This is one of the most common and easily-fixed causes of unreliable few-shot outputs.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def ask(prompt: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=30,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return repr(resp.content[0].text.strip())


QUERY = "The update fixed the login bug."

# Bad: inconsistent field names and delimiter styles
inconsistent = f"""Input: The app crashed on startup.
Label: Negative

Text: Works great!
Sentiment - Positive

Message: Delivery took too long.
Result = Neutral

Input: {QUERY}
Label:"""

print("Inconsistent:", ask(inconsistent))
# Model may output any format — 'Positive', 'Sentiment - Positive', 'Result = Positive', etc.


# Good: identical structure in every example
consistent = f"""Text: The app crashed on startup.
Label: Negative

Text: Works great!
Label: Positive

Text: Delivery took too long.
Label: Neutral

Text: {QUERY}
Label:"""

print("Consistent:  ", ask(consistent))
# Reliably outputs exactly one label word on the same line
Must be identical across all examples
Using "Label:" in example 1 and "Answer:" in example 2
Single blank line or "---" — pick one
Some examples separated by one line, others by two
Mixed case across examples; model reproduces the mixture

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


class FewShotPrompt:
    """
    Ensures format consistency by generating all examples
    from the same template.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        input_field: str = "Text",
        output_field: str = "Label",
        separator: str = "\n\n",
    ):
        self.input_field  = input_field
        self.output_field = output_field
        self.separator    = separator
        self._examples: list[tuple[str, str]] = []

    def add(self, text: str, label: str) -> "FewShotPrompt":
        self._examples.append((text, label))
        return self

    def _format_example(self, text: str, label: str = "") -> str:
        result = f"{self.input_field}: {text}\n{self.output_field}:"
        if label:
            result += f" {label}"
        return result

    def build(self, query: str) -> str:
        parts = [self._format_example(t, l) for t, l in self._examples]
        parts.append(self._format_example(query))  # no label — model fills it
        return self.separator.join(parts)


# All examples rendered through the same template — no inconsistency possible
builder = (
    FewShotPrompt(input_field="Text", output_field="Label", separator="\n\n")
    .add("Absolutely love this product!",    "Positive")
    .add("Broken on arrival — very upset.",  "Negative")
    .add("Does the job. Nothing more.",       "Neutral")
)

test_queries = [
    "Shipped fast, exactly as described.",
    "Instructions are missing from the box.",
    "Worst purchase of the year.",
]

for query in test_queries:
    prompt = builder.build(query)
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    label = resp.content[0].text.strip()
    print(f"{label:10s}  {query}")

Format consistency is the cheapest reliability improvement available in few-shot prompting: it costs nothing to fix and the gains are immediate. The practical approach is to generate all examples programmatically from a single template rather than writing them by hand — hand-written examples almost always have small inconsistencies (a missing space, a different capitalisation, an extra newline) that are invisible to a human reader but that the model detects and may reproduce. A template enforces consistency structurally, not by inspection.
